project_information.project_title: Santa Rosa Downtown Pedestrian Safety Improvements – Phase II
project_information.project_applicant: City of Santa Rosa Public Works Department
project_information.project_location_specific: Fourth Street between B Street and E Street, including intersections at Fourth & D and Fourth & E, Santa Rosa, CA 95401
project_information.description_of_nature_purpose_and_benefi:
The project consists of minor public right-of-way improvements along approximately three blocks of Fourth Street in downtown Santa Rosa, generally between B Street and E Street. Work includes installation of high-visibility crosswalk striping, curb extensions (bulb-outs) at three intersections, ADA-compliant curb ramps, replacement of a limited number of damaged sidewalk panels, installation of pedestrian-scale LED lighting on existing poles, and the addition of two curbside bus stop landing pads to improve accessibility for transit users.

The primary purpose of the project is to enhance pedestrian safety and accessibility in a heavily used downtown commercial corridor that serves local residents, employees, students, seniors, and visitors. The corridor includes ground-floor retail shops, restaurants, small offices, and upper-floor residential units. The project responds to a documented collision history involving pedestrians in crosswalks at the Fourth & D and Fourth & E intersections, and it implements measures identified in the City’s adopted Downtown Station Area Specific Plan and Bicycle and Pedestrian Master Plan.

Beneficiaries of the project include:
- Local residents who walk to nearby services, restaurants, and employment.
- Transit riders using Sonoma County Transit and Santa Rosa CityBus who will gain safer, more accessible boarding areas.
- Individuals with disabilities who will benefit from ADA-compliant curb ramps, smoother sidewalk transitions, and improved lighting.
- Local businesses that are expected to see increased foot traffic due to an improved pedestrian environment and perceived safety.
- Visitors to downtown events and the nearby SMART commuter rail station, who will experience more intuitive and visible pedestrian crossings.

No new travel lanes, building expansions, or changes in land use are proposed. All work is confined to existing public right-of-way and previously disturbed urban surfaces (asphalt and concrete). There will be short-term, temporary construction-related impacts such as localized noise and minor traffic detours, but these will be managed through standard best management practices and will not result in long-term environmental impacts. The improvements are consistent with existing zoning and adopted City plans for the area and are intended to bring existing pedestrian infrastructure up to current safety and accessibility standards rather than increase capacity or induce growth.
project_information.name_of_public_agency_approving_project: City of Santa Rosa City Council

exemption_status.categorical_exemption_state_type_and_sec: Class 1, Existing Facilities (CEQA Guidelines §15301(c)); Class 4, Minor Alterations to Land (CEQA Guidelines §15304(h))
exemption_status.reasons_why_project_is_exempt:
The project qualifies for a Categorical Exemption under the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) pursuant to CEQA Guidelines Section 15301, Class 1 (Existing Facilities), and Section 15304, Class 4 (Minor Alterations to Land).

Under Section 15301(c), the project involves minor alterations of existing public streets, sidewalks, and related facilities with no expansion of use beyond that existing. All proposed work—installation of high-visibility crosswalks, construction of curb extensions and ADA-compliant curb ramps, replacement of damaged sidewalk panels, and addition of pedestrian-scale lighting on existing poles—occurs entirely within the existing, improved public right-of-way along an already developed downtown commercial corridor. The project does not add new vehicle travel lanes, increase roadway capacity, or change the functional classification of the street. Instead, it upgrades and restripes existing facilities to improve safety and accessibility.

Under Section 15304(h), the project also qualifies as a minor alteration to land in that it consists of minor, temporary, and small-scale improvements to existing transportation corridors, such as the addition of bus pads and curb extensions, that do not involve the removal of healthy, mature, scenic trees or any grading beyond localized excavation necessary for curb, ramp, and sidewalk construction. Any landscaping disturbed by construction will be replaced in-kind or with similar drought-tolerant, non-invasive species consistent with the City’s approved street tree and landscape palette.

The project site is located in a fully urbanized downtown setting characterized by existing multi-story commercial and mixed-use buildings, paved streets, and utilities. There are no waterways, wetlands, or sensitive biological habitats within or adjacent to the project limits. The area is not within a designated floodplain or habitat conservation area, and it is not mapped as a Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone.

The project does not trigger any of the exceptions to the use of categorical exemptions outlined in CEQA Guidelines Section 15300.2. Specifically:
1. **Location** – The project is not located in an environmentally sensitive area such as a wetland, riparian corridor, or habitat for rare or endangered species. The site is fully developed urban right-of-way.
2. **Cumulative Impact** – The project consists of limited safety and accessibility upgrades and does not represent one of a series of related projects that, when viewed cumulatively, would result in significant environmental impacts. Similar pedestrian safety projects in the downtown area are small in scale and primarily involve striping and curb ramp upgrades.
3. **Significant Effect Due to Unusual Circumstances** – The project involves typical pedestrian and accessibility improvements in a downtown context and does not present unusual circumstances that could reasonably be expected to result in significant environmental effects. The work is similar to routine maintenance and minor capital improvements regularly undertaken by the City in developed areas.
4. **Scenic Highways** – The project is not located along a designated state scenic highway.
5. **Hazardous Waste Sites** – The project area is not identified on a list of hazardous waste sites compiled pursuant to Government Code Section 65962.5.
6. **Historical Resources** – While portions of downtown Santa Rosa include historic-era buildings, the project is limited to surface improvements within the street and sidewalk right-of-way. No alterations to building façades or historic structures are proposed. All excavation will be shallow and confined to previously disturbed soils, and the work will not materially impair the significance of any historical resource as defined by CEQA.

For these reasons, the City of Santa Rosa has determined that the project is exempt from CEQA under the cited categorical exemptions, and no further environmental review is required.

The primary purpose of the project is to enhance pedestrian safety and accessibility in a heavily used downtown commercial corridor that serves local residents, employees, students, seniors, and visitors. The corridor includes ground-floor retail shops, restaurants, small offices, and upper-floor residential units. The project responds to a documented collision history involving pedestrians in crosswalks at the Fourth & D and Fourth & E intersections, and it implements measures identified in the City’s adopted Downtown Station Area Specific Plan and Bicycle and Pedestrian Master Plan.

Beneficiaries of the project include:
- Local residents who walk to nearby services, restaurants, and employment.
- Transit riders using Sonoma County Transit and Santa Rosa CityBus who will gain safer, more accessible boarding areas.
- Individuals with disabilities who will benefit from ADA-compliant curb ramps, smoother sidewalk transitions, and improved lighting.
- Local businesses that are expected to see increased foot traffic due to an improved pedestrian environment and perceived safety.
- Visitors to downtown events and the nearby SMART commuter rail station, who will experience more intuitive and visible pedestrian crossings.

No new travel lanes, building expansions, or changes in land use are proposed. All work is confined to existing public right-of-way and previously disturbed urban surfaces (asphalt and concrete). There will be short-term, temporary construction-related impacts such as localized noise and minor traffic detours, but these will be managed through standard best management practices and will not result in long-term environmental impacts. The improvements are consistent with existing zoning and adopted City plans for the area and are intended to bring existing pedestrian infrastructure up to current safety and accessibility standards rather than increase capacity or induce growth.

grounding_evaluation.ground_truth:
The project qualifies for a Categorical Exemption under the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) pursuant to CEQA Guidelines Section 15301, Class 1 (Existing Facilities), and Section 15304, Class 4 (Minor Alterations to Land).

Under Section 15301(c), the project involves minor alterations of existing public streets, sidewalks, and related facilities with no expansion of use beyond that existing. All proposed work—installation of high-visibility crosswalks, construction of curb extensions and ADA-compliant curb ramps, replacement of damaged sidewalk panels, and addition of pedestrian-scale lighting on existing poles—occurs entirely within the existing, improved public right-of-way along an already developed downtown commercial corridor. The project does not add new vehicle travel lanes, increase roadway capacity, or change the functional classification of the street. Instead, it upgrades and restripes existing facilities to improve safety and accessibility.

Under Section 15304(h), the project also qualifies as a minor alteration to land in that it consists of minor, temporary, and small-scale improvements to existing transportation corridors, such as the addition of bus pads and curb extensions, that do not involve the removal of healthy, mature, scenic trees or any grading beyond localized excavation necessary for curb, ramp, and sidewalk construction. Any landscaping disturbed by construction will be replaced in-kind or with similar drought-tolerant, non-invasive species consistent with the City’s approved street tree and landscape palette.

The project site is located in a fully urbanized downtown setting characterized by existing multi-story commercial and mixed-use buildings, paved streets, and utilities. There are no waterways, wetlands, or sensitive biological habitats within or adjacent to the project limits. The area is not within a designated floodplain or habitat conservation area, and it is not mapped as a Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone.

The project does not trigger any of the exceptions to the use of categorical exemptions outlined in CEQA Guidelines Section 15300.2. Specifically:
1. **Location** – The project is not located in an environmentally sensitive area such as a wetland, riparian corridor, or habitat for rare or endangered species. The site is fully developed urban right-of-way.
2. **Cumulative Impact** – The project consists of limited safety and accessibility upgrades and does not represent one of a series of related projects that, when viewed cumulatively, would result in significant environmental impacts. Similar pedestrian safety projects in the downtown area are small in scale and primarily involve striping and curb ramp upgrades.
3. **Significant Effect Due to Unusual Circumstances** – The project involves typical pedestrian and accessibility improvements in a downtown context and does not present unusual circumstances that could reasonably be expected to result in significant environmental effects. The work is similar to routine maintenance and minor capital improvements regularly undertaken by the City in developed areas.
4. **Scenic Highways** – The project is not located along a designated state scenic highway.
5. **Hazardous Waste Sites** – The project area is not identified on a list of hazardous waste sites compiled pursuant to Government Code Section 65962.5.
6. **Historical Resources** – While portions of downtown Santa Rosa include historic-era buildings, the project is limited to surface improvements within the street and sidewalk right-of-way. No alterations to building façades or historic structures are proposed. All excavation will be shallow and confined to previously disturbed soils, and the work will not materially impair the significance of any historical resource as defined by CEQA.

For these reasons, the City of Santa Rosa has determined that the project is exempt from CEQA under the cited categorical exemptions, and no further environmental review is required.

It involves no new travel lanes, no change in functional classification, and no expansion of use.

activity_or_event_description.describe_the_activity_or_event_and_the_l: Small group photography and hiking tours including 'Waterfalls & Forest Light – DuPont Explorer' and 'Lake Light & Quiet Paths'. Activities involve hiking and photography at Hooker Falls, High Falls, Triple Falls, and Lake Imaging Access Areas.

insurance_information.liability_insurance_company_name: Blue Ridge Outdoor Risk & Liability Insurance Co.

activity_or_event_description.describe_the_activity_or_event_and_the_l:
We are applying for a commercial use permit to operate small-group, guided hiking and nature photography outings within DuPont State Recreational Forest, primarily focused on waterfall and fall-foliage experiences for out-of-town visitors. Our company, Blue Ridge Adventure Guides LLC, is a locally owned guiding service based in Asheville, NC, specializing in low-impact, educational outdoor experiences for adults and families.

The specific activity proposed is a half-day guided hiking and photo-tour called “Waterfalls & Forest Light – DuPont Explorer.” Each outing will be approximately 4–5 hours in duration and will include:

1) A safety briefing at the trailhead (including Leave No Trace principles, trail etiquette around other user groups such as mountain bikers and equestrians, and specific guidance on staying behind protective railings near waterfalls).
2) A moderate hike on established, designated trails to several of DuPont’s well-known scenic features, with frequent stops for photography, natural history interpretation, and short rest breaks.
3) A structured photography and observation segment near one major waterfall where guests are guided on how to frame shots without leaving the trail or trampling vegetation.
4) A return hike via the same established trails back to the designated access area.

Primary locations within the forest we wish to use include:

- Hooker Falls Access Area: We plan to start many of our tours from the Hooker Falls parking area due to its good facilities and clear signage. From there, we will use existing trails to visit Hooker Falls and Triple Falls overlooks. For groups with adequate fitness and time, we may also continue on to the High Falls overlook, staying on marked trails at all times.

- High Falls Access Area: On days when Hooker Falls parking is at capacity, or for slightly more advanced groups, we plan to begin from the High Falls Access Area. From this trailhead, we will use the main multi-use trails to reach the High Falls and Triple Falls viewing areas, remaining on designated viewing platforms and existing hardened surfaces.

- Lake Imaging Access Area (limited use): For a small number of outings, especially in peak fall color season, we may begin at Lake Imaging to offer a quieter forest and lake-edge photography experience. In these cases, we will utilize only established multi-use trails and will not leave designated paths.

Group structure and logistics:
- Participants will be transported to the forest in their own personal vehicles or via pre-arranged carpooling. We will not be operating shuttle buses or large vans inside the forest.
- Each group will be led by one professional guide who holds current Wilderness First Aid certification and CPR training. For groups larger than 8 participants, we will add a second assistant guide to help manage pace and safety.
- We will limit our group size on foot to a maximum of 10 paying participants plus up to 2 guides (12 people total) to minimize crowding on trails and at viewpoints.

Educational and interpretive component:
- During the hikes, we will provide interpretive talks on DuPont’s natural and cultural history, including the geology that creates the waterfalls, the forest’s mixed hardwood ecosystem, seasonal wildflowers, and basic wildlife awareness.
- The photography element is focused on helping visitors capture better landscape and waterfall images using whatever camera they have (smartphones or DSLR). We will explicitly instruct guests not to enter the water at the top of waterfalls, not to cross protective railings, and not to climb on slick rocks.

Safety and environmental protections:
- All tours will follow established trails only; we will not create social trails or lead guests off-trail.
- We will carry a small first-aid kit, extra water, and a printed map of DuPont State Recreational Forest on each outing.
- We will strictly enforce a pack-in/pack-out policy for all trash, including food wrappers and tissues. Guides will perform a brief sweep of rest areas and viewing points before leaving to ensure no litter is left behind by our group.
- We will avoid particularly busy times at the main waterfall viewpoints when possible (e.g., mid-day on peak fall-color weekends) and adjust our timing or route to reduce congestion and user conflict.

Overall, this is a recurring, low-impact, guided hiking and photography program aimed at visitors who want a structured, safe, and educational way to experience DuPont’s scenery. We are not proposing any races, competitive events, amplified music, staging areas beyond normal trailhead use, or any temporary structures. All activities will be completed within daylight hours, and we will comply with all posted rules and any additional conditions attached to the commercial use permit.

grounding_evaluation.ground_truth:
We are applying for a commercial use permit to operate small-group, guided hiking and nature photography outings within DuPont State Recreational Forest, primarily focused on waterfall and fall-foliage experiences for out-of-town visitors. Our company, Blue Ridge Adventure Guides LLC, is a locally owned guiding service based in Asheville, NC, specializing in low-impact, educational outdoor experiences for adults and families.

The specific activity proposed is a half-day guided hiking and photo-tour called “Waterfalls & Forest Light – DuPont Explorer.” Each outing will be approximately 4–5 hours in duration and will include:

1) A safety briefing at the trailhead (including Leave No Trace principles, trail etiquette around other user groups such as mountain bikers and equestrians, and specific guidance on staying behind protective railings near waterfalls).
2) A moderate hike on established, designated trails to several of DuPont’s well-known scenic features, with frequent stops for photography, natural history interpretation, and short rest breaks.
3) A structured photography and observation segment near one major waterfall where guests are guided on how to frame shots without leaving the trail or trampling vegetation.
4) A return hike via the same established trails back to the designated access area.

Primary locations within the forest we wish to use include:

- Hooker Falls Access Area: We plan to start many of our tours from the Hooker Falls parking area due to its good facilities and clear signage. From there, we will use existing trails to visit Hooker Falls and Triple Falls overlooks. For groups with adequate fitness and time, we may also continue on to the High Falls overlook, staying on marked trails at all times.

- High Falls Access Area: On days when Hooker Falls parking is at capacity, or for slightly more advanced groups, we plan to begin from the High Falls Access Area. From this trailhead, we will use the main multi-use trails to reach the High Falls and Triple Falls viewing areas, remaining on designated viewing platforms and existing hardened surfaces.

- Lake Imaging Access Area (limited use): For a small number of outings, especially in peak fall color season, we may begin at Lake Imaging to offer a quieter forest and lake-edge photography experience. In these cases, we will utilize only established multi-use trails and will not leave designated paths.

Group structure and logistics:
- Participants will be transported to the forest in their own personal vehicles or via pre-arranged carpooling. We will not be operating shuttle buses or large vans inside the forest.
- Each group will be led by one professional guide who holds current Wilderness First Aid certification and CPR training. For groups larger than 8 participants, we will add a second assistant guide to help manage pace and safety.
- We will limit our group size on foot to a maximum of 10 paying participants plus up to 2 guides (12 people total) to minimize crowding on trails and at viewpoints.

Educational and interpretive component:
- During the hikes, we will provide interpretive talks on DuPont’s natural and cultural history, including the geology that creates the waterfalls, the forest’s mixed hardwood ecosystem, seasonal wildflowers, and basic wildlife awareness.
- The photography element is focused on helping visitors capture better landscape and waterfall images using whatever camera they have (smartphones or DSLR). We will explicitly instruct guests not to enter the water at the top of waterfalls, not to cross protective railings, and not to climb on slick rocks.

Safety and environmental protections:
- All tours will follow established trails only; we will not create social trails or lead guests off-trail.
- We will carry a small first-aid kit, extra water, and a printed map of DuPont State Recreational Forest on each outing.
- We will strictly enforce a pack-in/pack-out policy for all trash, including food wrappers and tissues. Guides will perform a brief sweep of rest areas and viewing points before leaving to ensure no litter is left behind by our group.
- We will avoid particularly busy times at the main waterfall viewpoints when possible (e.g., mid-day on peak fall-color weekends) and adjust our timing or route to reduce congestion and user conflict.

Overall, this is a recurring, low-impact, guided hiking and photography program aimed at visitors who want a structured, safe, and educational way to experience DuPont’s scenery. We are not proposing any races, competitive events, amplified music, staging areas beyond normal trailhead use, or any temporary structures. All activities will be completed within daylight hours, and we will comply with all posted rules and any additional conditions attached to the commercial use permit.

applicant_details.please_state_why_you_are_leaving_this_ad:
I am looking to move primarily because my current apartment has become too small and increasingly noisy for my needs, and the building itself is beginning to show its age in ways that affect day-to-day living.

I’ve been in my current one-bedroom unit for a little over two and a half years. When I first moved in, it was a good fit: relatively affordable, close to my job, and I didn’t mind the older finishes. Over time, however, several issues have compounded:

1. **Space and layout constraints**: I now work from home 3–4 days a week as a marketing analyst. I spend long stretches on video calls and need a quiet, dedicated workspace. My current place is essentially a small living room, a bedroom, and a galley kitchen. I’ve been using the dining nook as an office, which means my work setup is crammed between the kitchen and the living area. It’s not ideal for concentration, and it makes it difficult to separate work life from home life. I also recently started playing guitar more seriously and would like a corner where I can keep my equipment set up without it spilling into walkways.

2. **Noise and building environment**: Over the last year, the unit above me has turned over twice, and the most recent tenants tend to have late-night gatherings on weeknights. I’ve tried to handle it politely—knocking on their door, speaking with them directly, and then bringing it up with the property manager. While management has spoken to them, the noise has only improved temporarily. Given that I have early morning meetings with clients in different time zones, being woken up at 1–2 a.m. multiple times a month has become a real problem.

3. **Maintenance and building condition**: The building is older, and while the landlord has been generally responsive, the underlying issues are starting to show. For example, my bathroom ceiling has needed patching twice due to leaks from the upstairs unit, and while it gets repaired, it hasn’t been fully resolved at the source. The windows are also quite drafty in winter, which affects both comfort and heating costs. I’ve had to use plastic window film and additional space heaters to keep the apartment comfortable during the coldest months.

4. **Desire for a longer-term, more stable home**: I’m at a point in my life where I’d like to settle into a place I can see myself staying in for several years, rather than renewing year-to-year and hoping conditions don’t worsen. I’m specifically looking for a property that is better maintained, a bit quieter, and slightly larger—ideally with a more defined space that I can use as a home office. Your property looks like it would allow me to create a more stable and comfortable routine.

To be clear, I don’t have any disputes with my current landlord, and my rent is fully up to date. I’ve always paid on time (usually via online transfer before the first of the month), and I’ve taken good care of the unit. This move is really about finding a space that better fits my current work-from-home lifestyle, offers a bit more room to breathe, and provides a quieter, better-maintained environment where I can reasonably plan to stay long term.

entertainer_information.genre_style: Acoustic folk and pop (e.g., James Taylor, Fleetwood Mac, Ed Sheeran)

entertainer_information.music_background:
Canal City Acoustic is a Welland-based acoustic duo featuring Daniel Thompson (vocals, acoustic guitar) and Melissa Carter (vocals, light percussion). Daniel grew up in Niagara and started performing at open mics in high school, later playing rhythm guitar in several local cover bands that focused on classic rock and folk. He has about 12 years of experience playing regularly at coffeehouses, farmers’ markets, and small community festivals across the Niagara region. Melissa has a background in choir and musical theatre and spent much of her teens and early twenties singing in community choirs and church groups, where she developed strong harmony and blend skills. She also has experience leading sing-alongs and family-oriented events, which has helped shape the duo’s interactive, approachable style.

The two met in 2019 at an open mic night in downtown Welland and began collaborating on acoustic arrangements of familiar songs that work well in casual, family-friendly environments like markets and street festivals. Since forming Canal City Acoustic, they have performed regularly at the Welland Farmers’ Market during special event days, at the Canal Days celebrations in nearby Port Colborne (busker stages), and at several local cafés and wineries in the Niagara region, such as Flat Rock Cellars and small independent coffee shops in Welland and St. Catharines.

Their typical set list mixes acoustic versions of classic rock (The Beatles, Fleetwood Mac, Eagles), folk and roots (James Taylor, Joni Mitchell), and more recent pop and indie tunes (Ed Sheeran, Vance Joy, Adele) arranged for two voices and one guitar, with occasional light percussion (shaker, cajón) to keep the rhythm interesting but not overpowering for a morning market atmosphere. They focus on keeping volume and tone appropriate so that shoppers can still chat with vendors while enjoying the music in the background. They are fully self-contained with a compact PA system and are experienced in setting up quickly and adapting to different outdoor and indoor spaces.

In addition to performing, Daniel occasionally teaches beginner guitar to local teens and adults, and Melissa volunteers as a music leader for a children’s reading program, where she incorporates simple songs and call-and-response activities. These experiences have taught them how to read a crowd, adjust their set list on the fly, and maintain a positive, welcoming stage presence. Both are familiar with performing in variable weather conditions and are comfortable playing rain or shine, provided equipment can be kept dry and safe. They understand the expectations of the Jammin’ at the Market series, including bringing their own sound equipment, keeping content family-friendly, and being punctual and reliable for the 10am–11am performance window.
entertainer_information.genre_style: Acoustic folk-pop, classic rock, and light contemporary covers

secret_evaluations.detail_content: I’ve been playing solo acoustic sets around Niagara since 2012, but I really committed to music full-time after my dad, Richard Thompson, had a minor stroke in March 2018 and I took leave from a warehouse job at the GM plant in St. Catharines to help him through rehab at the Welland Hospital for about 6 months. During that period I picked up more paid gigs at the Welland Farmers’ Market and local cafés to cover the roughly $900 a month we were short on his prescriptions and my lost income, and that’s when I realized I could make music my primary work.

secret_evaluations.detail_content: Most of my acoustic arranging skills came from a 10-week anxiety group I did at the Niagara Mental Health & Wellness Centre in Spring 2017, where my therapist, Dr. Karen Patel, had me bring my guitar to each Tuesday session at 3:00 pm to work on turning my racing thoughts into simple chord progressions. I ended up writing and refining about 15 short pieces there that later became the backbone of my solo market sets.

request_details.general_purpose_of_request_attach_receip:
Reimbursement for team pre-season training gear and healthy post-game meals for the MTHS Girls Varsity Soccer team.

Over the past six weeks (late November through early January), I, Sarah Mitchell (team parent and Booster member), purchased a combination of training equipment and food items for the girls’ soccer program with the understanding from Coach Ruiz and the Booster Board that these would be reimbursed from the Girls Soccer Booster funds once receipts were submitted.

1. Training pinnies and cones:
- Purchased 24 reversible numbered scrimmage pinnies (red/yellow) so that we could consistently run full-field 11v11 scrimmages plus subs without players having to share or swap during drills. Prior to this purchase, we only had 10 usable pinnies, which made scrimmages confusing and uneven. These pinnies are labeled with numbers to help the coaching staff track player positioning during film review and training.
- Purchased 40 additional disc cones and 12 tall coaching cones used for agility ladders, dribbling drills, and setting up small-sided games. Our old cones were cracked and frequently blew away in the wind, which slowed down practice transitions and reduced the number of reps the girls could get in each session.
- These items have already been used at three indoor pre-season practices at the MLT Recreation Pavilion and at two outdoor conditioning sessions at the high school field, and they are now part of the team’s shared equipment inventory.

2. Goalkeeper training gloves and resistance bands:
- Purchased two pairs of mid-range, high-grip goalkeeper gloves for our two primary keepers. Our returning varsity keeper had been using a pair that was over a year old with the latex peeling off, which was affecting her confidence and performance, especially in wet conditions. The new gloves are being stored with the team equipment and will remain with the program for future seasons.
- Purchased a 5-pack set of resistance bands (varying resistance levels) used during warm-ups and injury-prevention routines (particularly for hip and knee stability exercises). These have been incorporated into the pre-practice dynamic warm-up and are used by both varsity and JV players.

3. Post-game healthy meals and snacks for winter league matches:
- For three winter league games hosted at MTHS (on 2025-12-02, 2025-12-16, and 2026-01-06), I purchased post-game food for the full roster (approximately 22 players plus 2 coaches). The focus was on healthier options to support recovery: turkey and veggie wraps, fruit (bananas, clementines, grapes), granola bars, string cheese, and bottled water. I also purchased a few gluten-free and dairy-free options to accommodate specific player dietary needs that were communicated at the beginning of the season.
- In each case, I coordinated with Coach Ruiz ahead of time to confirm dates, expected player counts, and any updated dietary restrictions. After each game, we laid out the food on a folding table near the main bleachers so players could eat immediately after cool-down and stretching.
- Receipts are attached from Fred Meyer (Lynnwood location) and Costco (Shoreline location), clearly labeled by date and game opponent (Shorewood, Meadowdale, and Everett).

4. Small incidentals:
- Included in the receipts are minor incidental items that were purchased specifically for team use: paper plates, napkins, hand sanitizer, and disposable gloves used when handling and serving food. These were necessary to keep the serving area clean and to comply with basic food-safety practices, especially given that players often come directly off the field and head straight to the food tables.

All receipts have been scanned and attached in chronological order with handwritten notes in the margins indicating which purchases correspond to which practice or game. The total amount requested below is the sum of all eligible team-related expenses; any non-team personal items on the original store receipts have been carefully crossed out and excluded from the reimbursement calculation.

Coach Ruiz reviewed this list of expenses via email on 2026-01-08 and confirmed that they align with the approved Girls Soccer Booster budget for pre-season training and player support. I am requesting that the reimbursement check be issued to me personally (details below) so that I can reconcile my personal credit card, which was used for these purchases.
